lynx.response:
Here is a concise technical orientation for the **Deep Codex** project.

```json
{
  "primary_language": "Swift (100%, macOS native)",
  "entry_points": [
    "Sources/DeepCodex/App.swift — @main struct DeepCodexApp (SwiftUI App lifecycle, macOS target 14+)",
    "Sources/DeepCodex/MenuBarManager.swift — MenuBarManager.setup() (menu bar icon/mode switcher)",
    "Sources/DeepCodex/AppState.swift — AppState.init() (central state controller orchestrating all actions)"
  ],
  "hotspots": [
    "AppState.parseJson (complexity 8, fan-in 5) — parses `current-mode.json` to drive UI state",
    "AppState.runShell (complexity 4, fan-in 9) — shell execution hub called by all mode-switching actions",
    "AppState.refreshStatus (complexity 4, fan-in 4) — full system status refresh entry point",
    "AppState.detectLatestThreads (104 lines, god component) — SQLite + filesystem fallback thread detection",
    "AppState.continueToTargetMode (44 lines, god component) — copy context + switch mode + open Codex",
    "ContextExporter.runCommand (fan-in 8) — python3/sqlite3 shell runner used by all message extractors",
    "MenuBarManager.Shell.run (fan-in 10) — shell execution from menu bar mode switcher",
    "AppState.debugLog (fan-in 10) — uses throughout for diagnostics",
    "ContentView (862 lines, god component) — largest file: main dashboard UI with cards, tools, capabilities",
    "ContinueWorkModal (675 lines, god component) — modal for thread selection, context generation, export"
  ],
  "relevant_tests": [],
  "summary": "Deep Codex is a macOS SwiftUI app that acts as a mode-switching dashboard for OpenAI's Codex Desktop app. It wraps three 'modes' — Normal (GPT-5.5 via OpenAI), DeepSeek V4 Pro, and DeepSeek V4 Flash (both via a local Moon Bridge proxy on port 38440). The app is composed of 9 source files (~3,600 lines total) split across: a central `AppState` controller (602 lines, tightest coupling), a `ContentView` dashboard (874 lines, god component), a `ContinueWorkModal` for portable context export (675 lines), a `ContextExporter` for reading Codex session JSONL files via Python3 helpers (401 lines), a `CompletionAlertManager` for polling 'agent-turn-complete' event files and playing alerts (348 lines), a `MenuBarManager` for NSStatusItem mode switching (193 lines), an `AdvancedCapabilities` model layer (163 lines), and a dark-theme `DeepTheme` (112 lines). The primary language is Swift; the project uses Swift 6 and SwiftUI with AppKit/NSViewRepresentable bridges, targets macOS 14 (Sonoma), and has zero tests. Key external integrations include shell scripts in `~/Developer/ai-tools/codex-mode/`, the Codex Desktop app bundle, a SQLite database at `~/.codex/state_5.sqlite`, and the Moon Bridge HTTP proxy. Twenty god-component warnings were detected, with `ContentView`, `ContinueWorkModal`, and `AppState` being the most critical refactoring candidates."
}
```

baseline.response:
Now I have a complete picture of the project. Here is the technical orientation:

```json
{
  "primary_language": "Swift (Swift 6, macOS 14+)",
  "entry_points": [
    "Sources/DeepCodex/App.swift — @main struct DeepCodexApp: App, the SwiftUI app entry point. Creates the AppState and ContentView.",
    "Sources/DeepCodex/AppState.swift — @MainActor ObservableObject centralizing all state, mode switching, shell execution, thread detection, alert config, and advanced capabilities.",
    "Sources/DeepCodex/MenuBarManager.swift — NSStatusItem-based menu bar icon and mode switcher, separate from the window lifecycle."
  ],
  "hotspots": [
    "Sources/DeepCodex/AppState.swift — Hub of all logic: mode switching (normal/deepseekV4Pro/deepseekV4Flash), Moon Bridge management, thread detection via SQLite+filesystem fallback, context generation orchestration, and alert configuration.",
    "Sources/DeepCodex/ContentView.swift — The main SwiftUI view with three action cards (mode switching), tools panel, alert controls, system info panel, and an advanced capabilities section showing web search, image input, and routing status.",
    "Sources/DeepCodex/CompletionAlertManager.swift — Polls ~/.codex/notifications/events for agent-turn-complete JSON files, plays sound alerts, and uses accessibility APIs to detect when the user returns to Codex/VSCode/Cursor (via mouse or keyboard events).",
    "Sources/DeepCodex/ContextExporter.swift — Read-only layer that parses Codex session JSONL files (via python3 subprocess) to extract user messages, assistant responses, agent messages, function calls, file paths, and errors. Generates portable Markdown context for cross-mode workflow continuation.",
    "Sources/DeepCodex/ContinueWorkModal.swift — Modal UI for the 'Continuar trabajo' feature: thread picker grouped by project folder, destination mode selector, context preview, and actions to copy or copy+switch+open Codex in the target mode.",
    "Sources/DeepCodex/AdvancedCapabilities.swift — Codable models for parsing JSON output from advanced-capabilities-status.sh, with display helpers for web search, image input, and routing status."
  ],
  "relevant_tests": [
    "No test files found in the project (no Tests directory, no test targets in Package.swift)."
  ],
  "summary": "DeepCodex is a macOS-native SwiftUI menu-bar companion app for OpenAI's Codex desktop IDE. It manages three modes (Codex Normal/GPT-5.5, DeepSeek V4 Pro, DeepSeek V4 Flash) by switching scripts and orchestrating a local proxy called Moon Bridge (port 38440). The app provides real-time status monitoring, completion alerts (via polling notification events and accessibility APIs), advanced capability inspection (web search, image input, routing), and a cross-mode 'continue work' feature that reads Codex session JSONL files via embedded python3 scripts, generates portable Markdown context, and switches modes seamlessly. No test targets exist."
}
```

lynx.response:
Here is my comprehensive onboarding assessment of the **DeepCodex** project.

```json
{
  "architecture": [
    "DeepCodex is a native macOS 14+ (Sonoma) SwiftUI+AppKit desktop companion app for OpenAI's Codex desktop client. It has no external SPM dependencies — only Apple frameworks (SwiftUI, AppKit, AVFoundation, OSLog, ApplicationServices).",
    "The app follows a monolithic view-state architecture: a single @MainActor class (`AppState`) acts as the ObservableObject central store, injected into a single `ContentView` that renders all UI. The MenuBarManager runs as a separate singleton with its own polling loop.",
    "External shell scripts in `~/Developer/ai-tools/codex-mode/` are the system integration layer: status.sh, codex-switch.sh, start-moonbridge.sh, advanced-capabilities-status.sh, and restore-last-backup.sh. The app shells out via Foundation's Process class.",
    "Core subsystems: (1) Mode switching — toggles between Normal (OpenAI GPT), DeepSeek V4 Pro, and DeepSeek V4 Flash via Moon Bridge proxy; (2) Completion alerts — polls a notifications/events directory for JSON event files, plays looping sounds, detects user return via mouse/keyboard events; (3) Context export — reads Codex JSONL session files via Python one-liners, generates portable Markdown for cross-mode continuation; (4) Menu bar — NSStatusItem with mode indicator dot and menu.",
    "There are 155 nodes and 523 edges in the code graph. 7 structural clusters exist: (1) codex/open — mode switching actions, (2) extract/messages — ContextExporter pipeline, (3) start/alert — CompletionAlertManager lifecycle, (4) detect/latest — thread detection, (5) display/color — menu bar UI, (6) generate/markdown — Markdown generation, (7) thread/row — ContinueWorkModal UI."
  ],
  "entry_points": [
    "`DeepCodexApp` @main struct in Sources/DeepCodex/App.swift — the SwiftUI app entry point. It creates an AppState and renders ContentView in a windowGroup with hidden title bar.",
    "`AppState.init()` — initializes the global state, kicks off Moon Bridge port check (checkMoonBridgeDirect), loads advanced capabilities, and restores alert sound preferences from UserDefaults.",
    "`MenuBarManager.setup(with:)` — called on ContentView.onAppear. Creates the NSStatusItem, builds the menu, and starts a 2-second polling loop for mode changes.",
    "`CompletionAlertManager.startWatching()` — called from AppState.refreshStatus(). Starts a 1-second polling timer for agent-turn-complete JSON events in the events directory."
  ],
  "important_modules": [
    "**AppState** (Sources/DeepCodex/AppState.swift, 602 lines) — The central god object. Manages all state (activeMode, moonBridgeStatus, detectedThreads, alert state), all shell commands (runShell, runCommand), mode switching, context detection, and JSON parsing. 18 hotspot functions. Fan-in of 17 from other classes.",
    "**ContextExporter** (Sources/DeepCodex/ContextExporter.swift, 401 lines) — Pure read-only logic for reading Codex JSONL session files. Embeds Python one-liners as inline scripts to parse JSONL (extracting model, provider, user/assistant/agent messages, function calls, file paths, errors). Generates the portable Markdown context document. The most testable module in the project.",
    "**CompletionAlertManager** (Sources/DeepCodex/CompletionAlertManager.swift, 348 lines) — File-system-based polling service for completion events. State machine: inactive → sounding → attended. Plays looping sounds, detects user return via global NSEventMonitor (mouse move, key press) when Codex/VS Code/Cursor is frontmost. Configurable sound and minimum task duration.",
    "**ContentView** (Sources/DeepCodex/ContentView.swift, 874 lines) — Massive monolithic SwiftUI view containing: header (logo + mode info), 3 action cards (Normal/Pro/Flash), status panel, tools panel with 6 tools, system info panel, and the advanced capabilities expandable section (web search, image input, routing cards). All rendered in a single scroll view with auto-height window sizing.",
    "**ContinueWorkModal** (Sources/DeepCodex/ContinueWorkModal.swift, 675 lines) — Modal sheet with a 7-phase state machine (loading, empty, selecting, generating, ready, exporting, done, error). Orchestrates thread selection → context generation → copy/switch workflow."
  ],
  "hotspots": [
    "**ContentView.swift (874 lines)** — God component. A single View struct with 25+ subviews and functions. Couples UI rendering, business logic (actionCard, toolItem), and state observation in one file. Extremely hard to navigate or test.",
    "**AppState.swift (602 lines, fan-in=17)** — God class. Combines shell execution, JSON parsing, mode switching, alert configuration, advanced capabilities polling, thread detection (23+ complexity), and context export in one object. The Mode enum has fan-in=20, making it a coupling bottleneck.",
    "**detectLatestThreads (complexity=23, 104 lines)** — The most complex function in the project. Contains two fallback strategies (SQLite JSON parsing → filesystem find+grep) with nested error handling, multiple debugLog calls, and complex string manipulation. High risk of bugs.",
    "**parseJson (complexity=8, fan-in=5)** — Central JSON parser for current-mode.json. Called after every mode switch and refresh. Has 8 distinct execution paths through optional chaining and type casting.",
    "**runShell / runCommand (fan-in=9-10 each)** — Primitive shell execution wrappers used everywhere. Duplicated across AppState (2 versions) and ContextExporter (1 version) and MenuBarManager.Shell (1 version). All 4 implementations differ slightly in bash/zsh choice and error handling — a maintenance risk.",
    "**generateMarkdown (126 lines)** — Generates the portable context document in a single function. Mixes string interpolation, conditional branching for empty/non-empty sections, and truncation logic. A clear candidate for template-based generation."
  ],
  "test_observations": [
    "**ZERO tests exist in the entire project.** There are no test targets in Package.swift, no XCTest imports, no test files anywhere. The code graph contains exactly 1 TESTS edge (likely an auto-detected annotation). This is the most significant quality gap.",
    "**ContextExporter is the most testable module** — all methods are static, pure (except for shelling out to Python), and have deterministic inputs/outputs. The Python one-liners could be mocked by injecting a JSONL string instead of a file path. A unit test target for extractUserMessages, extractAssistantMessages, extractFunctionCalls, and generateMarkdown would catch parsing errors and regressions quickly.",
    "**CompletionAlertManager's state machine** (inactive → sounding → attended) is well-defined and isolated enough for unit testing with mocked timers and file system access. The sound playback side-effect should be abstracted behind a protocol for testability.",
    "**AppState is extremely hard to test** due to tight coupling to Process (shell execution), FileManager, NSWorkspace, and UserDefaults. The shell wrappers (runShell, runCommand) would need to be injected or replaced. The `detectLatestThreads` function with its dual-strategy fallback logic (SQLite → filesystem) is particularly fragile and untestable without heavy mocking.",
    "**Missing tests for:** (1) ContextExporter's Python script correctness (JSONL format changes in Codex could silently break extraction); (2) CompletionAlertManager's edge cases (permissions denied, corrupted event files, rapid successive completions); (3) Mode switching race conditions (concurrent isBusy guard, quitCodexDesktopApp timing); (4) Thread detection's SQLite vs filesystem fallback behavior when provider mismatches; (5) Markdown generation with empty/edge-case inputs.",
    "**The smart review** flagged critical complexity issues in AppState (cyclomatic complexity 103 on auto-generated hash/== methods from the ThreadInfo struct) and flagged \"no tests in this directory\" for every file reviewed."
  ],
  "evidence": [
    "Project stats: 10 Swift files, 155 graph nodes, 523 edges, 102 methods, 30 classes, avg cyclomatic complexity 8.87, avg cognitive complexity 21.19.",
    "Package.swift defines a single executable target named DeepCodex with no test target and no dependencies.",
    "No test files found via grep for XCTest, no Tests directory, no test files in graph search.",
    "5 god components detected: ContentView (874 lines), ContinueWorkModal (675 lines), AppState (602 lines), ContextExporter (401 lines), CompletionAlertManager (348 lines).",
    "20 god components total including sub-functions (generateMarkdown at 126 lines, detectLatestThreads at 104 lines).",
    "Graph reveals 180 CALLS edges, 88 READS edges, and only 1 TESTS edge.",
    "Code duplication: 4 different shell-execution wrappers (AppState.runShell, AppState.runCommand, ContextExporter.runCommand, Shell.run) with inconsistent argument handling and shell choice (bash vs zsh)."
  ],
  "summary": "DeepCodex is a macOS companion app that manages Codex mode switching (Normal/DeepSeek V4 Pro/DeepSeek V4 Flash), provides completion alerts via sound, and exports portable Markdown context for cross-mode work continuation. The architecture is monolithic: a single god object (AppState, 602 lines) drives a monolithic SwiftUI view (ContentView, 874 lines) plus a complex modal (ContinueWorkModal, 675 lines). The project has zero automated tests, 5 major god components, significant code duplication in shell-execution wrappers, and high structural coupling (Mode enum fan-in=20, AppState fan-in=17). The most urgent technical debt is the complete absence of tests — especially for ContextExporter (the most testable module) and CompletionAlertManager's state machine. The hottest hotspots are AppState.detectLatestThreads (complexity=23), ContentView.swift (874 lines), and the duplicated shell-execution utilities. Refactoring the god components, introducing dependency injection for Process/FileManager, adding a test target, and standardizing the shell-execution layer would dramatically improve maintainability."
}
```

baseline.response:
Now I have a thorough understanding of this project. Here is my comprehensive onboarding summary:

```json
{
  "architecture": [
    "DeepCodex is a macOS (14.0+) SwiftUI desktop app built with Swift 6.0 (compiled with 6.3.2). It is a menu-bar + window utility for managing ‘Codex’ (OpenAI’s coding assistant) profiles: switching between normal (OpenAI), DeepSeek V4 Pro, and DeepSeek V4 Flash modes via local shell scripts and a ‘Moon Bridge’ proxy service.",
    "The project uses no external dependencies—only Apple frameworks (SwiftUI, AppKit, Combine, AVFoundation, ApplicationServices, OSLog, Foundation). It is a single executable target in a SwiftPM package (package name: ‘source’).",
    "Architecture is monolithic: a single module with ~10 source files. The design follows a classic SwiftUI MV-like pattern: AppState (ObservableObject) is the centralized state manager, injected into ContentView and ContinueWorkModal via @ObservedObject. MenuBarManager is a separate singleton NSObject for the NSStatusBar menu.",
    "Window management is handled by a custom WindowConfigurator (NSViewRepresentable) and a WindowSizer singleton that reads content height via SwiftUI PreferenceKey and animates frame resizing.",
    "The app is dark-mode-only with a custom color/gradient theme defined in Theme.swift. The entry point is a custom-named function (DeepCodex_main) linked via -Xfrontend -entry-point-function-name."
  ],
  "entry_points": [
    "Sources/DeepCodex/App.swift – The @main struct DeepCodexApp, conforming to SwiftUI.App. It creates AppState via @StateObject and presents ContentView inside a WindowGroup with hidden title bar and content-sized resizability.",
    "The actual C-level entry point is DeepCodex_main (a renamed main() via compiler flag), which is mapped to the standard _main by a linker alias (-Xlinker -alias _DeepCodex_main _main). This is found in the build command in .build/release.yaml and .build/plugin-tools.yaml."
  ],
  "important_modules": [
    "Sources/DeepCodex/AppState.swift (601 lines) – Central nervous system of the app. Contains all @Published state (activeMode, moonBridgeStatus, detectedThreads, advancedCapabilities, alert system state). Implements all action methods: mode switching, shell execution (runShell/runCommand), JSON parsing, debug logging, thread detection, and context export.",
    "Sources/DeepCodex/ContentView.swift (873 lines) – The main SwiftUI view. A complex layout with headerSection, actionCards, bottomSection (toolsPanel + systemInfoPanel), and an expandable advancedCapabilitiesSection. Uses PreferenceKey (ContentHeightKey) for dynamic window sizing. Triggers MenuBarManager.setup() on appear.",
    "Sources/DeepCodex/ContinueWorkModal.swift (674 lines) – A full-featured sheet modal for the ‘continue work in another mode’ feature. Has complex state machine (ModalPhase enum: loading → empty/selecting → generating → ready/exporting → done/error). Manages thread selection, destination mode selection, context generation via ContextExporter, and action buttons (copy only vs copy+switch).",
    "Sources/DeepCodex/CompletionAlertManager.swift (347 lines) – ObservableObject that watches a filesystem events directory for Codex task completion files, plays alert sounds (infinite loop AVAudioPlayer), and auto-dismisses on user interaction (mouse movement, key presses, or frontmost app focus) in approved bundle IDs.",
    "Sources/DeepCodex/ContextExporter.swift (400 lines) – Pure-logic struct for reading Codex session JSONL files via python3 subprocess invocations. Extracts user messages, assistant messages, agent messages, function calls, file paths, and errors. Generates comprehensive markdown context blocks for continuing work across modes.",
    "Sources/DeepCodex/MenuBarManager.swift (191 lines) – NSObject singleton managing the NSStatusBar item. Displays current mode with colored dots/emojis, builds a dynamic NSMenu with mode-switching items, and polls state changes via a polling loop.",
    "Sources/DeepCodex/Theme.swift (111 lines) – All design tokens: colors (bgTop/bgMid/bgBottom, accent colors for each mode, text colors, border colors), gradients (bgGradient, logoGradient, per-mode cardGradient), and dimension constants. Contains Color/NSColor hex initializers.",
    "Sources/DeepCodex/AdvancedCapabilities.swift (162 lines) – Codable model structs (AdvancedCapabilities, V1ModelsInfo, CodexWebSearchInfo, MoonBridgeWebSearchInfo, ImageInputInfo, RoutingInfo) plus display helper extensions. Parses the output of advanced-capabilities-status.sh."
  ],
  "hotspots": [
    "AppState.swift lines 559–584 – runShell and runCommand methods execute arbitrary shell scripts (/bin/bash, /usr/bin/sqlite3, /usr/bin/find, /usr/sbin/lsof, custom script paths under ~/Developer/ai-tools/codex-mode/). These are fragile: hardcoded paths, no sandboxing, synchronous Process with read-to-end-of-file pattern risking deadlocks on large outputs.",
    "AppState.swift lines 319–421 – detectLatestThreads runs sqlite3 with raw SQL constructed via string interpolation (risk of injection from provider name). Falls back to filesystem enumeration via find+grep across ~/.codex/sessions/. This is a complex, high-latency operation that runs synchronously in a detached Task.",
    "ContentView.swift lines 19–51 – The body view hierarchy is extremely deeply nested (fixedSize, multiple modifiers, preference readers, conditional content). This is a hotspot for SwiftUI rendering performance and maintainability.",
    "ContinueWorkModal.swift – A massive monolithic view (~674 lines) with many sub-views (emptyView, doneView, errorView, loadingView, generatingView, exportingView, sourceInfoSection, threadPickerSection, destinationPickerSection, previewSection, warningSection, actionButtons). The phase-switching logic in contentForPhase uses a large switch statement. Thread grouping logic (threadGroups computed property) runs Dictionary(grouping:) with sorting on every access.",
    "CompletionAlertManager.swift – Relies on Accessibility permissions (AXIsProcessTrusted), a polling timer (1s interval for file watching, 0.5s for stop condition), a global event monitor for key/mouse events, and filesystem-based IPC (watching eventsDir for agent-turn-complete-*.json files). This is inherently fragile and battery-intensive.",
    "ContextExporter.swift – All extraction methods shell out to /usr/bin/python3 with inline Python scripts passed via -c. This is a major portability and performance hotspot; it fails silently on any error (bare except: pass).",
    "MenuBarManager.swift – Uses a polling loop (2s interval Task with sleep) to detect state changes instead of Combine publishers, and observes NSApplication.didBecomeActiveNotification as a proxy.",
    "The project has a custom entry point (DeepCodex_main) aliased to main, which is unusual and introduces linker-level complexity."
  ],
  "test_observations": [
    "There are ZERO test targets in Package.swift – no unit tests, no UI tests, no test discovery commands exist in the build description.",
    "The entire project has no Tests/ directory or test files whatsoever.",
    "Given the critical nature of the functionality (shell execution, file I/O, JSON parsing, state management), the lack of tests is a significant risk. In particular: runShell/runCommand error handling, JSON decoding of external files, ContextExporter Python script outputs, and the modal state machine are all untested.",
    "The only automated verification present is compile-time (Swift 6 strict safety) and the build succeeds (binary exists at .build/arm64-apple-macosx/release/DeepCodex).",
    "The CompletionAlertManager's testAlert() method creates a synthetic event file for manual testing but is not automated.",
    "High-priority candidates for test coverage: (1) ContextExporter parsing and markdown generation, (2) AppState runShell/runCommand edge cases, (3) AdvancedCapabilities JSON decoding, (4) ModalPhase state transitions, (5) ThreadInfo model logic."
  ],
  "evidence": [
    "Package.swift shows .executableTarget(name: 'DeepCodex', resources: [.process('Resources')]) with zero dependencies, zero test targets.",
    ".build/release.yaml and .build/plugin-tools.yaml confirm the linker alias: -Xlinker -alias -Xlinker _DeepCodex_main -Xlinker _main and -Xfrontend -entry-point-function-name -Xfrontend DeepCodex_main.",
    "Swift version from .build/swift-version--1AB21518FC5DEDBE.txt: 'Apple Swift version 6.3.2 (swiftlang-6.3.2.1.108 clang-2100.1.1.101) Target: arm64-apple-macosx26.0'.",
    "The sources file list (from .build/arm64-apple-macosx/release/DeepCodex.build/sources) contains exactly 10 source files (Theme, AppState, ContinueWorkModal, App, MenuBarManager, CompletionAlertManager, ContextExporter, AdvancedCapabilities, ContentView, plus a derived resource_bundle_accessor.swift).",
    "Build output-file-map.json confirms whole-module-optimization (WMO) with 10 compilation units.",
    "AppState.swift line 559-570 shows the synchronous Process-based shell execution pattern with a known pipe deadlock comment at line 579.",
    "ContextExporter.swift lines 31-48, 52-69, 73-96, etc. all use equivalent python3 subprocess patterns.",
    "CompletionAlertManager.swift line 72 checks AXIsProcessTrusted() for Accessibility permissions."
  ],
  "summary": "DeepCodex is a macOS menu-bar + window utility for managing Codex AI assistant profiles. It is a single-module Swift 6.0 app with zero external dependencies and zero tests. The app lets users switch between Codex Normal (OpenAI), DeepSeek V4 Pro, and DeepSeek V4 Flash modes by running local shell scripts, checking a Moon Bridge proxy process, monitoring task completion events via filesystem polling, and exporting conversation context (as markdown) to continue work across modes. The codebase is characterized by heavy reliance on subprocess execution (bash, python3, sqlite3, find, lsof), synchronous Process I/O, filesystem-based IPC, and Accessibility permissions for alert auto-dismissal. The UI is a single-window dark-mode SwiftUI layout with a custom theme, dynamic window sizing, and a complex modal sheet for context export. The absence of automated tests, the fragile shell-out patterns, the polling-based monitoring, and the deeply nested SwiftUI views are the principal technical risks."
}
```
